coredata.dc:title: Toxic heavy metal ion detection by fluorescent nanocarbon sensor derived from a medicinal plant

coredata.dc:description: In the twenty-first century, the importance of environmental pollution sensing cannot be overstated. Cadmium is a well-known poisonous heavy metal that seriously endangers human health. In terms of screening for poisons and diagnosing illnesses, the sensitive and focused detection of cadmium in cells is crucial. In this work, we developed Green fluorescent Carbon nanomaterial (Carbon nanomaterial) synthesized from a novel precursor, Justicia Wynaadensis, by the most eco-friendly, cost-effective hydrothermal method, which acts as a fluorescent probe for Cadmium fluorescence sensing technique with the concentration range of 1 nM–1 M. The sensor displays remarkable linear detection with a 5.235 nM detection limit.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Metal ion sensing
Green synthesis

Metal ion preparation of various concentrations of Cd2+ as analyte
Structure and morphological analysis of the synthesised sample
Optical properties of synthesised carbon nanomaterial

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD pattern (b) Raman spectra of the synthesised carbon nanomaterial WS-10.
Photostability of the WS-10 sample up to 10 h of exposure.
(a) UV–vis spectra of bare sample and WS-10 sample (b) Photoluminescence (PL) spectra of WS-10 sample.
Stokes shift spectra between the absorption and fluorescence emissions of WS-10 sample indicating large stokes shift.

(a) PL emission spectra of WS-10 exhibiting the fluorescence quenching with the interaction of Cd2+ at various concentration ranges (b) Linear response of the fluorescence quenching of WS-10 displayed in the Stern-Volmer plot.
Fluorescence lifetime decays of WS-10 nanomaterial in the absence (blackline) and presence (redline) of Cd2+ analyte.
Fluorescence lifetime measurement of WS-10 sample.

head.ce:title: Toxic heavy metal ion detection by fluorescent nanocarbon sensor derived from a medicinal plant

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics and Electronics, Christ University, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics and Electronics, Christ University, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the twenty-first century, the importance of environmental pollution sensing cannot be overstated. Cadmium is a well-known poisonous heavy metal that seriously endangers human health. In terms of screening for poisons and diagnosing illnesses, the sensitive and focused detection of cadmium in cells is crucial. In this work, we developed Green fluorescent Carbon nanomaterial (Carbon nanomaterial) synthesized from a novel precursor, Justicia Wynaadensis, by the most eco-friendly, cost-effective hydrothermal method, which acts as a fluorescent probe for Cadmium fluorescence sensing technique with the concentration range of 1 nM–1 M. The sensor displays remarkable linear detection with a 5.235 nM detection limit.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Cadmium (Cd) is toxic that builds up over time, affects the skeletal and respiratory systems, and is linked to kidney disease. It is a hazardous heavy metal that has been under more and more regulation in recent years. The two main exposure routes for the general public are eating particular foods and smoking [1]. There may be additional exposure, though, given that the metal has historically and currently been used in consumer goods. Numerous regulatory organizations have classified the heavy metal and its compounds as known or probable non-threshold human carcinogens due to evidence connecting occupational exposure to lung cancer [2,3]. Cadmium, with an atomic weight of 112.41 and atomic number 48, belongs to group XII of the periodic table of chemical elements. This silvery-white metal is soft and chemically similar to zinc and mercury in its physical and chemical properties [4]. Hence a need to detect Cd is a crucial issue that is being faced right now. Due to their high sensitivity and selectivity, classical analytical methods must be used among the existing sensing techniques assisting nanoparticles in detecting harmful toxins at low concentrations. However, these analytical methods have limitations, such as time-consuming sample preparation, specialist equipment, preconcentration procedures, and the need for skilled personnel [5,6]. Contrarily, chemical sensors are frequently used to detect numerous harmful compounds close at hand. Due to its high selectivity, sensitivity, and low procedural expense, the laser spectrophotometry-based technique has proven extremely beneficial for metal ion sensing compared to other methods. Carbon-based nanodots are considered one of the possible sensory advanced materials among several traditional fluorophores. C-dots are a better contender for various applications in environmental clean-up due to their high stability toward different reaction mediums, photostability, biocompatible nature, and excellent solubility in aqueous media [7].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of nanocarbon from leaf extract
ce:section.ce:para: Justicia Wynaadensis (WS) was procured from the household backyard in Kodagu district, Karnataka. Carbon nanomaterials from WS were synthesized via one step, chemical-free hydrothermal synthesis route in a Teflon-lined autoclave at 180 °C for 10 h. The as-obtained sample was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 20 min, filtered using 0.1 μm, nylon membrane filter paper (CAT No. 170013 P), labelled, and stored as WS-10 for further characterizations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Metal ion preparation of various concentrations of Cd2+ as analyte
ce:section.ce:para: Cadmium salt was sourced from Merck India Ltd with a molecular weight (MW) of 236.42 g/mol. Various concentrations of Cd2+ ranging from 1 Molar (Molar) to 1 nM (Nano-Molar) were prepared from successive dilution from the stock solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure and morphological analysis of the synthesised sample
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesized carbon nanomaterial WS-10′s XRD is represented in Fig. 1 (a), and it clearly shows the peak orientation at 2θ angles of 23.18° with the (002) lattice plane, demonstrating that the presence of N- and O-containing groups made the WS-10 an amorphous graphite in its initial state. These results are in line with those for carbon nanomaterial that have already been reported [8,9]. These results indicate that the WS-10 carbon source was effectively recovered and transformed into carbon nanomaterial.

The Raman spectrum of the synthesized carbon nanomaterial in the spectral range of 500–3000 cm−1 is shown in the Fig. 1(b). The obtained spectra are deconvoluted into individual peaks to identify the presence of different functional groups. Comparing the standard data, it is found that the strong stretching vibration bands of CS, CNO2, CH and CC are observed at 683 cm−1,1364 cm−1,1459 cm-1and 1887 cm−1 respectively. The weak stretching vibration of COC is found at 835 cm−1. The presence of oxygen containing functional group is mainly responsible for the fluorescence of the carbon nanomaterial [10].

The WS-10 material's photostability has a significant impact on both its performance and applicability in fluorescence detection. Following a 10-hour exposure to light, the WS-10 fluorescence emission profile was evaluated in conformity with this. The fluorescence emission intensity versus time graph, which indicates that there is hardly any observable photo-bleaching on exposures up to 10 h, confirms the outstanding photostability of the created WS-10 nanocarbon sensor material (Fig. 2 ). Due of its outstanding photostability, WS-10 is a potential material for applications across many disciplines, especially fluorescence sensing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of synthesised carbon nanomaterial
ce:section.ce:para: The optical properties of WS-10 were analysed by UV − visible absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy. The UV − visible absorption spectra of WS-10 and the precursor leaf extract (Bare) were recorded in the range of 200–750 nm Fig. 3 (a). The WS-10 showed a broad and strong absorption band at 265.81 nm, attributed to the π–π* transition of the aromatic CC bonds. In contrast, the bare sample showed a strong absorption at 413.77 nm and a weak absorption peak at 595 nm. This remarkable difference between the spectrum of WS-10 and the spectrum of leaf extract (Bare) may be due to the synthesized sample's carbonization and alterations in the chemical composition [11].

The fluorescence emission spectra of JW-10 are obtained for various excitation wavelengths from 510 to 610 nm Fig. 3(b). An excitation-independent PL characteristic was observed, with emission peaks centred at 600 nm without any significant shift. This characteristic may be because the optical band gap of the material is higher than the excitation wavelengths, which leads the emission wavelengths to peak around the emission wavelength corresponding to the energy gap value of the carbon core [12].

Most fluorescent materials have a small Stokes shift that is caused by reabsorption by the fluorophore due to the significant overlap between the absorption and emission spectra. Although the development of fluorescent nanomaterials has made it possible to significantly minimise self-absorption, aggregated or concentrated nanocarbon materials still exhibit fluorescence attenuation, which lowers the sensitivity of biosensors. Self-absorption is the process by which a small stokes shift (50 nm) that causes fluorescence emission is absorbed by the fluorophore itself. This phenomenon also quenches the fluorescence that has a significant impact on the fluorophores' optical performance. Furthermore, aggregation-induced quenching (ACQ) or concentration quenching of fluorophores can also be triggered by self-absorption small stokes shift [13–15]. As a result, the relatively small Stokes shift, particularly for the sensing ensembles, limits the employment of small Stokes shift-based fluorophores in opto-sensing. Therefore, boosting the Stokes shift can significantly enhance the effectiveness of biosensing using nanocarbon materials. Nanomaterials with a large Stokes shift (>100–300 nm) are better suited for very sensitive bioassays [16]. Fig. 4 shows the Stokes shift spectra between the WS-10 sample's fluorescence emission and absorption. This sample exhibits a large Stokes shift (111.63 nm), which prevents self-absorption and reduces the spectrum overlap between the fluorophore's absorption and emission. Thus, it can be established that the synthesised carbon nanomaterial is perfect for fluorescence-based sensing given the larger stokes shift value of sample WS-10.

The fluorescence quenching efficiency of WS-10 with Cd2+ was observed using the fluorescence response of the WS-10 sensor to concentrations of Cd2+, ranging from 1 M to 1 nM. Fig. 5 (a) The WS-10 sample, with PL emission exhibiting the maximum emission at 570 nm (λmax), was employed [17,18] for the successive addition of various concentrations of selected metal ions, and the corresponding fluorescence spectra were recorded. The stern-Volmer plot corresponding to the WS-10 fluorescence sensor displays a linear response to the fluorescence quenching of the sample with a limit of detection (LOD) as low as 5.235 nM Fig. 5(b). The linear response thus employs the dynamic quenching mechanism that can be attributed to the resonance energy transfer or electron transfer [19–22] between the fluorescent sample WS-10 and Cd2+, the analyte material.

Green fluorescent carbon nanomaterial WS-10 synthesized from a novel precursor, Justicia Wynaadensis, by the most eco-friendly, cost-effective hydrothermal method, which acts as a fluorescent probe for Cadmium fluorescence sensing technique. The carbon nanomaterial showed outstanding optical properties and had a detection limit of 5.235 nM. Moreover, the entire preparation method of Carbon nanomaterial is a straightforward and green method. The results indicate that the synthesized carbon nanomaterial can have a substantial impact on changes in bio imaging and food security. Doping the Carbon nanomaterial with various elements which can bring changes in the detection limit can be done as a future work of this research.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Cadmium exposure in living organisms: A short review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhang, Hao, and Mindy Reynolds. “Cadmium exposure in living organisms: A short review.”Science of the Total Environment678 (2019): 761-767.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Environmental cadmium exposure and pancreatic cancer: Evidence from case control, animal and in vitro studies

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Djordjevic, Vladimir R., David R. Wallace, Amie Schweitzer, Novica Boricic, Djordje Knezevic, Slavko Matic, Nikola Grubor et al. “Environmental cadmium exposure and pancreatic cancer: Evidence from case control, animal and in vitro studies.”Environment international128 (2019): 353-361.

Daniel, and Manoj Balachandran. “Investigation of fluorescence enhancement and antibacterial properties of nitrogen-doped carbonized polymer nanomaterials (N-CPNs).”International Journal of Polymer Analysis and Characterization27, no. 7 (2022): 464-476.
